tickets.title: §21.7 — Coexistence: build the grouped serving surface (two lenses over one hyperschema)
tickets.body:
IMPLEMENTATION-READY (design landed 2026-07-16, #114 — §21.7 in spec/21-schema-identity.md; implement to it, don't re-litigate). Build coexistence: two lenses (resolution Schemas) served over one hyperschema, per-lens at every door, with the degenerate single-lens case BYTE-IDENTICAL.

THE SHAPE (pinned by §21.7 — dedup is a data structure, not a discipline): derive ONE grouped serving surface — programs: Map<hyperschemaName, { hyperschema, roots: UNION of member bindings' roots, lenses: Map<lensName, binding> }> — computed in ONE place from the surviving bindings; every consumer iterates the groups, none sees the flat Bound[] list. Consumers: SchemaRegistry.build takes one hyperschema per group; the reactor registers ONE materialization per program over the union roots; GraphQL builds one family per LENS (types <LensName>View/<LensName>Patch, query/subscription field, mutation family, hooks dispatch keyed on lens name); REST's path segment is the lens; loam.public admits lens by lens; the §17 version ladder runs per (registration entity, lens).

THE GROUPING KEY is read from the binding's own bytes: lens name = the `schema` pointer's target id minus the `schema:` prefix (in the bytes since slice 2prime). Latest-wins narrows to latest-PER-LENS — registering FilmClassic no longer evicts Film. A pre-coexistence store regroups conservatively (one lens name per hyperschema = one group = today's reading).

REFUSALS AT THE SEAM: (a) two bindings naming one hyperschema with DIFFERENT bodies (termHash mismatch) are refused at grouping, loudly, before any state changes; (b) two lens names legalizing to the same GraphQL identifier are already refused by the existing collision checks (gql.ts:336) — verify the refusal now guards the lens namespace. REBIND RULE: a new lens whose roots are a SUBSET of its program's union rides the additive path; one that WIDENS the union forces a generation rebind (the reactor has no deregister). Decide by comparing unions at the seam.

NO §20 MIGRATION — §21.7's on-wire verdict is nothing breaks (no delta grows/loses a role, no entity id renamed); the register input's one non-default is schema.name naming the lens, defaulting to hyperschema.name (today's behavior, forever the degenerate case).

RAILS THE TICKET DEMANDS: byte-identical single-lens SDL/OpenAPI/introspection/REST paths (snapshot-compare against main); latest-per-lens (register two lenses, both serve, each evolves alone); the grouping refusals (same-name-different-body; same-legalized-name); the rebind rule (subset-rooted additive, widening rebinds); per-lens §17 versions; per-lens loam.public (publishing Film reveals nothing of FilmClassic — uniform 404); writability honesty (a field writable through one sibling lens is writable — §14's posture, assert it as BEHAVIOR, not as a bypass-guard).

CODE ANCHORS: registration.ts:661 (the latest-wins take), registration.ts:556-564 (survivingCandidates grouping), gateway.ts:525-619 (fixpoint + additive path + rebind — the flat-list consumption sites), gateway.ts:1447-1449 (matName), gql.ts:309-311/334-340 (name keying + collision refusal), src/surface/rest.ts:49-73.

VILLAGE: an act where one hyperschema carries two living lenses — the broad public reading and a narrow archival one — served simultaneously, evolving on their own clocks, one publicly declared and one not.

FUTURE (not this ticket): VersionedHyperSchema (Film@termHash — build when a pin needs the gather frozen).

MERGE: no capability/auth/federation/erasure surface and no migration, but it rewires the gateway's core serving loop — if the classifier hesitates, it is Myk's merge (P6).

tickets.title: §27 — Membership is a query, first-class: select/watch + composable set-algebra scoping
tickets.body:
BUILD-READY. The first §27 implementation slice (design DECIDED and landed — #110/#112; the 0.6.0 substrate landed — #113): make a container's membership a first-class delta-query, exactly §27.6's decided shape (a rhizomatic Term → dset, static or live, local or remote).

DO: (1) Expose the evaluation as a gateway surface — select(term): evaluate a JSON op-profile Term (parseTerm → evalTerm, dset-sort required) over this store's ground, once; watch(term): the same, live (re-evaluated on pulse — a parameterized offeredDeltas, which spec/27.6 calls 'nearly free'). Refuse a non-dset Term loudly at the door. (2) Generalize the quarantine's seeding edge: QuarantineOptions gains a membership Term alternative to the admit predicate (admit stays as the degenerate case, per §24.10 — same knob, generalized, no new mechanism); the inbound federation filters the pulse by the Term. (3) Prove the composable scoping 0.6.0 bought: a pool seeded over a difference ('author X's deltas MINUS container Y's members'), including a NESTED difference, live-follows correctly — the exact composition the depth-1 select(not(inView(...))) idiom could never express.

BOUNDARY (0.6.0, pinned in §24.10 — do not fight it): difference/intersect are Term-layer operators ONLY; they may NOT ride inside inView predicates (depth-1 stratification unchanged). Compose scopes at the seeding/reading EDGE; invent no predicate-level machinery, no parallel vocabulary.

DO NOT BUILD (each its own later ticket): the module manifest (§27.6 question 1 — still OPEN, needs design); Merkle-set identity (§27.6 question 2); trust-on-load (§27.6 question 3); the full Container lifting with the five-knob vector (§27.7 — comes when a SECOND policy wants the object); scope-merge's container-entity exclusion claims (needs the container-as-entity model); promote-LAW (§24.4).

RAILS: select returns exactly the Term's dset over a real ground; watch follows a pulse; a membership-Term-seeded pool admits exactly the members (and its erasure fan-out still holds — reuse the §24.8 rail against a Term-seeded pool); the nested-difference scope evaluates through the seeding edge; a non-dset Term is refused.

CODE ANCHORS: quarantine-pool.ts (QuarantineOptions.admit — the degenerate form being generalized); test/spike/set-algebra.test.ts (the 0.6.0 operators' proven reach, reuse its idioms); gateway.federate + offeredDeltas (the pulse being filtered); UNRUNNABLE_KEYS at src/federation/translate.ts:105 (difference/intersect need NO entry — they are Term ops, recorded there; do not add one).

VILLAGE: an act where the operator stands up a trial pool over a hand-picked scope — one author's claims minus an excluded container — watches it live-follow, and drops it. MERGE: touches the federation/quarantine seeding surface → Myk's merge (P6), never self-merge.

tickets.title: §24.8 — The erasure fan-out must re-derive its own reach (audit-2 HIGH + two MED)

edges.contract: T19 moves the erase/promote/quarantine bodies into their own module as a PURE MOVE. T16's fan-out fix (the closed-trust evasion, the nested pools, the seeding filter) must already be in those bodies, so the move carries a correct implementation instead of relocating a known-broken one — and T16's surgical diff never has to rebase across a 2,000-line restructure.

tickets.body:
BUILD-READY, NON-DESIGN. Audit 2 (2026-07-16, the erasure-law angle over the composed §8×§11×§24 system) found three defects in the §24.8 erasure fan-out. They are ONE unit — every one is the same mistake, that the fan-out TRUSTS a condition instead of RE-DERIVING it — and they exist only at the intersection of three PRs, which is why per-PR review could not see them. The §24.8 law is non-negotiable: erase in the primary, and the forgotten byte is gone from every attached pool, byte-for-byte, or the operator LEARNS the erasure did not complete. Today it can silently not complete.

FINDING 1 (HIGH) — a pool with a `closed` trust policy silently evades erasure, and `erase()` reports success.
`eraseReplica` lands the tombstone via `this.federate([tombstone])` with NO explicit admit (gateway.ts:1373), so the POOL'S OWN trust policy applies (`admitFor()`, gateway.ts:1460). Trust mode `closed` admits NOTHING — deliberately including the operator's own deltas (a documented, legitimate posture: src/gateway/trust.ts:8-9,27). SEQUENCE: primary declares trust `closed` -> `openQuarantine()` seeds the pool while its ground is still empty (policy defaults open, so the closed declaration federates in and, being operator-authored, BINDS there) -> `primary.erase(d)` -> the fan-out's tombstone is REJECTED at the pool's federate door -> the guard `if (!readTombstones(...).has(id)) return;` (gateway.ts:1378) takes the SILENT RETURN branch -> no purge, no throw. `erase()` resolves cleanly while d's bytes stay resolvable in the pool (and in any primary-union-pool opt-in scope). This defeats §24.8 asserts 2-4 AND contradicts spec/24's own residual note, which promises the fan-out is 'best-effort-and-LOUD — a pool whose eraseReplica throws makes erase REJECT' (spec/24-quarantine.md:444-447). This path does not throw.
THE FIX, AND WHY IT IS SAFE: the silent-return guard is CORRECT for a FORGED tombstone (slice 1's prosecute catch, railed in test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts) but it CONFLATES 'unauthorized' with 'trust-refused'. These are different layers and the code already separates them: `admit` is TRUST POLICY (whose data do I want), `eraseDefect` is AUTHORIZATION (is this a lawful operator tombstone) and runs INSIDE federate independent of admit (gateway.ts:1478-1479). So: pass an explicit admit override for the operator's tombstone (trust policy is not the erasure law's business — the pool is the operator's OWN replica, §24.1, and §11 reaches through the glass UNCONDITIONALLY), and if the tombstone still did not land, THROW — do not return silently. The forged-tombstone rail must still pass unchanged: eraseDefect, not admit, is what refuses a forgery. VERIFY THAT CLAIM against the code before building; if admit turns out to be load-bearing for the forgery refusal, STOP and surface it — that would be news.
DECIDED, so the builder does not have to guess (coldstart caught the tension): once admit is overridden, the ONLY remaining cause of a tombstone not landing is `eraseDefect` refusing it — i.e. the tombstone is UNLAWFUL. Those two cases take OPPOSITE answers and `eraseReplica` must tell them apart EXPLICITLY rather than inferring from `readTombstones`:
  - UNLAWFUL tombstone (eraseDefect returns a defect — a forged or foreign order, i.e. a hostile direct caller):     refuse WITHOUT purging. It may be loud (a throw is arguably better than a silent no-op for a caller who handed     over a forgery) but it MUST NOT purge, and rail (c) must still prove the byte survives untouched. If you make it     throw, update rail (c) to assert BOTH the throw AND the untouched byte — the byte assertion is the invariant, the     throw is the ergonomics.
  - LAWFUL tombstone that still did not land (an IO/backend failure inside federate, now the only other path): THROW,     so `erase` rejects and the operator learns the erasure did not complete — the documented 'best-effort-and-loud'     contract.
The shape that falls out: check `eraseDefect` FIRST and return/throw on a defect without purging; then federate with the admit override; then if the tombstone still is not in `readTombstones`, THROW. Never a silent success.

FINDING 2 (MED) — nested pools are outside the fan-out.
`erase()` fans out only to `this.quarantinePools` (gateway.ts:1239) and `eraseReplica` purges its own backend but NEVER iterates ITS OWN `quarantinePools` (gateway.ts:1372-1381). Nothing prevents nesting: a pool's `.gateway` is a full Gateway, `openQuarantine` is public, and the pool is operated (shared seed), so `pool.gateway.openQuarantine()` succeeds. SEQUENCE: P -> Q -> R all hold d; `P.erase(d)` purges P and Q; R keeps the byte, silently, no error, no tombstone. R is a LIVE IN-PROCESS replica, so this is distinct from the accepted durable-pool boot residual.
RECOMMENDED FIX: make the fan-out TRANSITIVE (eraseReplica recurses into its own pools) rather than refusing nesting. Recursion preserves the law regardless of what §24.2's tree clause eventually says about nested pools, and forecloses nothing; REFUSING `openQuarantine` on a pool would be a §24 design amendment (a (Myk) call), not a bugfix. If the recursion is built, guard against a cycle (a pool set that somehow contains an ancestor) — a fan-out that infinite-loops is a worse bug than the one being fixed.

FINDING 3 (MED) — a selective `admit` filter drops the primary's PRE-EXISTING tombstones at seeding.
`openQuarantine` applies `opts.admit` to the initial seed and every `reseed` (gateway.ts:1259-1261). A domain-shaped predicate (the §24.2 'hand-picked subset' knob, `QuarantineOptions.admit`, quarantine-pool.ts:40) rejects `loam.erasure` tombstones — they are just offered deltas. Erasures made WHILE ATTACHED still land (`eraseReplica` pushes the tombstone directly), but an erasure PREDATING attachment leaves the pool with NO tombstone for d — so `pool.gateway.federate([d])` from any lagging peer or saved offer RE-ADMITS the purged bytes (the pool's `dead` set at gateway.ts:1463 is empty for d), and an opt-in interop read over primary-union-pool resurfaces them. Breaks §24.8 asserts 3/4 for pre-attachment erasures.
FIX: the seeding edge passes operator tombstones through UNCONDITIONALLY, exactly as `eraseReplica` already does — wrap the caller's admit so a lawful tombstone is always admitted (`eraseDefect` inside federate still refuses a forged one). A quarantine must inherit the holes along with the ground; a membership filter narrows what the pool SEES, never what it must FORGET.

THE ONE-LINE DOCTRINE, worth stating in the code comment: A FAN-OUT MUST RE-DERIVE ITS OWN REACH. The purge re-checks the tombstone (slice 1's catch); promotion re-checks the shape, the closure, and the id (#111's catch); and now the fan-out re-checks that it actually reached every replica, and says so when it did not. Every one of these was the same bug: trusting a condition a caller or a config implied, instead of deriving it from what is recorded.

RAILS (each finding gets one that FAILS on today's code — write them first, watch them fail, then build):
  (a) closed-trust pool: primary declares trust `closed`, opens a pool, erases a delta -> the pool's backend holds ZERO       bytes of it (assert by content STRING at rest, not just by id — reuse the §24.8 composed-scope rail's idiom).
  (b) loud failure: a pool whose tombstone genuinely cannot land makes `erase` REJECT (the documented contract).       Construct this honestly — e.g. a pool whose backend/federate throws — do NOT assert it by mocking the very guard       under test.
  (c) forged tombstone STILL cannot purge — the slice-1 rail, whose BYTE assertion (the forged order leaves the byte       untouched) must survive verbatim; if the DECIDED shape above makes the refusal throw, extend this rail to assert       the throw too. This is the regression guard on the whole change: the fix removes a TRUST filter, never a CHECK.
  (d) nested pools: P -> Q -> R, erase in P, assert R's backend holds zero bytes of it.
  (e) admit-filtered seeding: erase d in the primary FIRST, THEN open a pool with a domain-shaped `admit` that would       reject a tombstone, then attempt `pool.gateway.federate([d])` -> refused, and the bytes are absent at rest.
  (f) the pool still admits only what `admit` selects for ordinary domain deltas (the narrowing knob is not broken by       the tombstone exception).

DO NOT: widen any authority (eraseDefect stays the only authorization gate — the fix removes a TRUST filter from the erasure path, never a CHECK); build the durable-pool boot re-attachment (spec/24-quarantine.md:308-316 names it as its own work, and it is a genuinely separate design); refuse nested quarantines (a §24 design amendment, Myk's call); touch §20 migration (see the deferral below); gold-plate the fan-out into a transaction (spec/24's 'best-effort-and-loud' is the DECIDED contract — this ticket makes it keep that promise, it does not upgrade it).

DEFERRED, NAMED (audit-2 finding 4, LOW — do not build here): `migrate` re-expresses an erased delta's content under a FRESH id (src/migrate/migrate.ts:403-426), so re-running a migration over a pre-erasure export can resurrect forgotten content past the id-based tombstone. Exposure is narrow (only the operator's own signature-verified DEFINITIONAL deltas; byte-identical data claims keep their ids and are refused correctly). It is a §20 surface, not the §24 fan-out, and its fix (migrate takes the store's tombstone set and skips re-expressing tombstoned inputs) is its own ticket + a spec/20 amendment. Named here so it is not lost.

CODE ANCHORS: `erase` fan-out loop gateway.ts:1239; `eraseReplica` gateway.ts:1372-1381 (the silent return at :1378, the un-overridden federate at :1373); `openQuarantine` + the reseed closure gateway.ts:1253-1274 (admit applied at :1260); `federate`'s layering gateway.ts:1452-1484 (dead-set :1463, eraseDefect :1478-1479, admit :1480, `admitFor()` :1460); trust modes src/gateway/trust.ts:8-9,27; `QuarantineOptions.admit` src/gateway/quarantine-pool.ts:40; existing rails test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts (7 — the forged-tombstone guard and the composed-scope byte-for-byte assertion are the two to preserve and imitate).

SPEC: spec/24-quarantine.md §24.8 gains the corrected contract (the tombstone crosses the glass regardless of the pool's trust policy and regardless of the seeding filter; the fan-out is transitive; failure is loud) + an implementation note on the landing PR. This is a CORRECTION to a built section — the rare edit-an-existing-file case, not a new spec file.

VILLAGE: extend demos/village/phase-quarantine.mjs (or a new act) so the village EXERCISES the law where it was silently broken: a CLOSED-TRUST primary with a NESTED pool (P -> Q -> R), erase once, and prove the byte is gone from every tier of the tree. That is the demonstrable half and it is the half that matters — the erasure now completes where it previously, silently, did not.
  NOT in the village (coldstart caught this as an unachievable DoD): 'show the operator being TOLD when a pool cannot   honor it'. After this fix a lawful tombstone can only fail to land via an IO/backend failure, which the village has   no honest way to stage — and inventing a fault-injection seam just to demo it is gold-plating. That path belongs to   rail (b), where a throwing stub backend can construct it honestly.

MIGRATION: none — no delta changes shape, no on-wire change; this is serving/fan-out logic only.

MERGE: capability/federation/ERASURE surface -> Myk's merge (P6), never self-merge, however green.

DONE WHEN: rails (a)-(f) are green (each having first failed on today's code, (c) excepted — it is the unchanged regression guard), `npm run check` is green with the count read aloud, spec/24 tells the truth about the corrected contract, and the village act runs.

tickets.title: Audit-2 honesty sweep: the SPEC.md index tells the truth, and the memory rail stops being hollow
tickets.body:
BUILD-READY, NON-DESIGN, no src/ behavior change. Audit 2's spec-vs-code angle found no case of the spec claiming a guard the code lacks (the dangerous direction is CLEAN). What it found is drift in the UNDERSTATING direction — the index and some labels describe a world from before this week's landings. SPEC.md is the record of what IS; an index that says a built thing is unbuilt is the router lying about the map.

1. (MED) SPEC.md's PREAMBLE contradicts what is built. SPEC.md:61-66 still says '§23 (renderers) is design-drafted — ... not yet built' and 'Reserved §24 (quarantine) is still in flight'; the row at SPEC.md:56 tags §24 '(design-stage draft)'. TRUTH: §23 is built across five merged slices (#99, #102-#105 — src/gateway/renderers.ts, bytes.ts, render-worker.ts, writeRoute) and §24 has slice 1 + promote-outputs merged (#109, #111 — src/gateway/quarantine-pool.ts, adopt.ts, Gateway.promote). Fix the preamble and the index rows to what IS, citing the landing PRs. Read each section's own Provenance footer for the truth rather than re-deriving it — the footers are correct; only the index is stale.

2. (LOW) Post-merge DRAFT labels are stale. spec/21-schema-identity.md:193 still heads §21.7 '(DRAFT — design-stage, pending Myk's P6; no provenance until it lands)' and spec/24-quarantine.md:456-457 says promote-outputs is 'open as #111, awaiting the same P6' / 'This whole section remains a DRAFT awaiting Myk's sign-off'. #111, #114 and #115 all MERGED on 2026-07-16 (commits 5daabeb, e90173d, 106e826). The P6 these labels await HAS HAPPENED. Convert them to their landed form with proper Provenance (the merge is the landing; §21.7 is now the accepted design whose IMPLEMENTATION is T2 — say exactly that, do not imply the code exists).
   CAREFUL — the honest distinction this ticket must preserve: §21.7 is DESIGN-LANDED but NOT BUILT. §24's promote-outputs is BUILT. Do not flatten the two into one word. A reader must still learn from spec/21 that coexistence is not yet in the code (T2 builds it), and audit-2's finding 2 is exactly that the §21 BODY reads present-tense ('The registry lifts the old 1:1 by keying on (hyperschema, schema-name)... so they coexist') while registration.ts:544-556,661-662 still groups by registration entity alone and evicts. Fix that body claim to name it as the design's contract awaiting T2 — the correctives currently sit hundreds of lines below it in §21.7 and the footer, which is too far to save a reader of the body.

3. (LOW, hollow test) test/gateway/render-sandbox.test.ts:86-96 ('a memory-hungry bundle is bounded') asserts ONLY `status === 500`, and its own comment concedes 'timed out OR reclaimed by resourceLimits'. The 500ms hard timeout alone produces that 500 — so the test passes IDENTICALLY if `resourceLimits` (src/gateway/render-worker.ts:70-74) were misconfigured or ignored, and spec/23-renderers.md:499's claim ('memory bounds so a bundle cannot OOM the host') has no test that distinguishes the memory ceiling from the timeout. The wiring DOES exist — this is a test-strength gap, not a refuted behavior. Strengthen it to assert the MEMORY bound specifically: a bundle that allocates hard should be reclaimed on the memory path, distinguishably from a timeout (e.g. assert the worker's exit/error signature, or that it dies FASTER than the timeout would allow). If — after honestly trying — the two paths prove indistinguishable from outside, that is a real finding: say so in the test comment and in spec/23, and DOWNGRADE the spec's claim to what is actually proven. Do not fake a distinction that isn't there; an honest 'this is bounded by the timeout, and resourceLimits is defense in depth we cannot observe from here' is worth more than a green assertion that proves nothing.

DO NOT: change any src/ behavior (except none — this ticket touches only SPEC.md, three spec sections, and one test); rewrite history in JOURNAL.md (append-only; audit-2 already has its entry); re-litigate any landed design.

MERGE: docs + one test, no behavior, no migration -> self-mergeable once `npm run check` is green AND P5 is clean. If the honest outcome of item 3 is a spec claim DOWNGRADE, that is a spec correction about a capability surface -> leave it for Myk (P6).

DONE WHEN: SPEC.md's index + preamble match what is built (verified against each section's Provenance footer); §21's body no longer reads as though coexistence works today; §24/§21.7's labels reflect the merges that happened; the memory rail either proves the memory bound or honestly says what it proves; `npm run check` green.

tickets.title: §23.9/§22.6 — cap the anonymous render fan, and make the resolver's declared type bind
tickets.body:
BUILD-READY, NON-DESIGN. Audit 2's executable-code-at-rest angle verified most of the surface CLEAN (data-URL framing cannot break out; cache keying is a content address; the worker timeout genuinely cannot wedge the main loop; no authority crosses postMessage; pins resolve by content address at serve time; the browser-bundle invariant test would really catch its regression). Two defects survived, unrelated to each other but both small and both in the executable-code surface — build them together or split; they share no code.

1. (MED) THE ANONYMOUS RENDER FAN IS UNCAPPED. Every rendered-route hit spawns a fresh worker_threads Worker with NO concurrency gate (src/gateway/render-worker.ts:66-97, `new Worker(...)` unconditional, resourceLimits up to 128MB old + 32MB young, 500ms lifetime), and the route is reachable ANONYMOUSLY with an attacker-chosen entity (src/server/http.ts:616-624). N concurrent anonymous requests force N live V8 isolates/OS threads holding up to ~160MB each — thread and memory exhaustion of the host. THE CODEBASE'S OWN STANDARD SAYS THIS SHOULD BE BOUNDED: the sibling anonymous SSE door caps in-flight work with `maxPublicStreams` (src/server/http.ts:313, default 256), and a render is STRICTLY more expensive per request than a stream (it spawns an OS thread). THIS IS NOT THE DOCUMENTED RESIDUAL: §23.9's accepted residual is 'a worker bounds hang/mem but is not ocap (no-fs/no-net)', and its 'warm pool' note concerns per-render SPAWN COST, not a concurrency CAP. FIX: an in-flight cap on concurrent renders, modeled on `maxPublicStreams` (same option shape, same refusal register, a sane default); over the cap, refuse cleanly (a 503-shaped refusal that leaks nothing) rather than queueing unboundedly. Decide and STATE whether the cap is global or per-door; the SSE cap is public-door-scoped — follow that precedent unless there is a reason not to, and say why in the comment. RAILS: the cap refuses the (N+1)th CONCURRENT render while N are genuinely in flight, and the host survives; an under-cap load still serves normally; a completed render RELEASES its slot (the leak-the-slot bug is the obvious way to get this wrong — rail it: run cap+1 renders SEQUENTIALLY and assert every one succeeds).

2. (MED) A RESOLVER'S DECLARED TYPE DOES NOT BIND, SO TWO DOORS DISAGREE. `applyResolvers` writes `fn(entries)` straight into the view with NO check that the returned shape matches the resolver's declared `type` (src/gateway/resolvers.ts:151-179, the unvalidated write at :170-176). §22.6's provenance EXPLICITLY promises this typing 'keep[s] the §17 two-doors-agree invariant' — the invariant is advertised and never enforced. SEQUENCE: an operator registers a rung-(a) resolver on field f declared `{ rung: 'a', type: 'string', code }` whose ESM returns an OBJECT. GraphQL types f as String (`resolverTypeOf`, src/gateway/gql.ts:192-214), `GraphQLString.serialize(object)` THROWS -> the field nulls with a serialize error. REST/`nodeBody` runs the same view through bytesEnvelope + JSON.stringify and emits the object VERBATIM (src/surface/rest.ts:258+). Same registration, same entity: GraphQL answers null+error, REST answers the object — a concrete §17 two-doors-DISAGREE, precisely the invariant §22.6 claims to preserve. (v1 runs only the OPERATOR'S own resolvers, so this is a stated-invariant break and a footgun, not a privilege breach — hence MED.) FIX: validate the resolver's return against its declared ResolverOutputType at the apply seam, ONCE, where every door inherits it — and on a mismatch do exactly what a THROWING resolver already does (fall back to the Policy value, per-field, blast radius of one field: resolvers.ts:170-176 already has that discipline — reuse it, do not invent a second failure mode). Include the `bytes` type (§23.7's envelope shape) in the validation. RAILS: a resolver declaring `string` and returning an object falls back per-field AND BOTH DOORS AGREE (assert the SAME answer from GraphQL and REST for the same entity — that is the invariant, assert it directly); each declared type accepts its own valid value; one bad resolver does not take down the view or the door.

DO NOT: build the warm worker pool (a performance slice, named and separate); attempt the no-fs/no-net ocap (§23.9's documented residual, its own large work); widen resolver rungs beyond (a) (§22's ladder is decided); change the throwing-resolver fallback semantics (reuse them).

SPEC: spec/23-renderers.md gains the render cap in §23.9's honest-scope paragraph (the residual list currently names the hang and the ocap; the CAP is now built — say so). spec/22-resolvers.md §22.6: the two-doors-agree claim becomes true — note WHEN it became true and what enforces it. Both are corrections to built sections (edit in place, implementation note on the landing PR).

VILLAGE: the render cap wants an act that hammers a public route and shows the door refusing cleanly while the host stays up.

MIGRATION: none — no delta changes shape.

MERGE: item 1 touches an anonymous-door resource discipline and item 2 an executable-code surface -> Myk's merge (P6).

DONE WHEN: the cap refuses the over-limit concurrent render and releases slots correctly; a mistyped resolver falls back and both doors agree; `npm run check` green with the count read aloud; both spec sections tell the truth.

tickets.title: Decompose the Gateway god-class: modularize the behaviors, keep the `this`

edges.contract: T2 rewires the serving loop (the grouped programs->lenses surface). After T19 that loop lives in ONE module behind a declared internals seam, so T2's diff is scoped to lifecycle/binding instead of landing in a 2,166-line class alongside erasure and HTML rendering.

T15 generalizes the quarantine seeding edge to a membership Term. After T19 the ingest-door and quarantine bodies are separate modules, so T15 touches the federation/quarantine module rather than the shared class body.

T18 caps the render fan and binds resolver output types. After T19 serveRoute/writeRoute/publishRenderer live in the renderer-serving module, so T18's diff no longer overlaps T2's and T16's edits to the same file.

tickets.body:
BUILD-READY, NON-DESIGN, ZERO BEHAVIOR CHANGE. `src/gateway/gateway.ts` is 2,166 lines and holds every concern the gateway has: `append`, `federate`, `erase`, `promote`, `openQuarantine`, `serveRoute`, `writeRoute`, `serveBytes`, `publishRegistration`, `register`/`rebind`/`reseat`, the §14 write verbs, reads/subscriptions, the public-door predicates, as-of, and the resolver memo. Erasure and HTML rendering share a `this`. Split it into modules by CONCERN, keeping the class and its `this` exactly as they are.

WHY (Myk, 2026-07-16 — get the reason right, because it decides the design): **the file is too big to REVIEW.** This is a readability and comprehension change, not a parallelism optimization. Width is a SIDE EFFECT and must never be the argument: an earlier analysis established that width is bounded by this file (every ticket collides here), but also that at width 1 the bottleneck is the human at P6, not the builder. So do not trade one gram of clarity for a scheduling win. If a boundary is good for a reader and bad for the forecast, take the reader's side.

THE MAP (measured 2026-07-16 against main; line numbers will drift as slices land — re-measure, do not trust these blindly). Seven concerns, and they fall almost cleanly:
  - LIFECYCLE & BINDING (~350): publishRegistration (:761, 118 lines), replayRegistrations (:523, 83), register (:714),     rebind (:606), reseat (:1387), open/boot, attachPersistence, preloadResolvers, assertTemplatesVisible (:439),     assertMaterializable, boundKey, matName/matFor. THE MOST ENTANGLED — do it LAST.
  - §14 WRITE VERBS (~250): mutateEntity (:1635), claimEntity (:1875), linkEntity (:1786), severEntity (:1826),     removeEntity (:1734), retract (:1687).
  - RENDERERS & SERVING (~250): writeRoute (:1093), serveRoute (:1006), publishRenderer (:929), serveBytes (:1160),     routeServableOn (:1072), appRouteOf, prepareRoute.
  - READS & SUBSCRIPTIONS (~200): watchEntity (:1933), subscribeVia (:2104), resolvedNode (:1607), resolvePinned (:387),     query, groundAsOf (:421), annotate (:427).
  - ERASURE · QUARANTINE · PROMOTION (~190): promote (:1286), erase (:1200), openQuarantine (:1253), eraseReplica,     adoptions. THE MOST ISOLATED — do it FIRST (and T16 lands its HIGH fix here before this ticket starts).
  - INGEST DOORS (~130): append (:634), federate (:1452), admitFor.
  - PUBLIC DOOR & VERSIONS (~100): surface (:341), isPublicLatest (:357), isPublicPin (:361), freezePublicEntry (:907),     declarePublic, registrationVersions (:368), withdrawnRegistrations (:374), hasPublicSurface.

MECHANISM (DECIDED — Myk, 2026-07-16: 'we can even keep the `this` if we just modularize the behaviors'): **THIN DELEGATING METHODS over free functions.** The class keeps every public method with its exact signature; the body moves to a concern module and the method becomes one line:
    async erase(id: string, opts: EraseOptions = {}): Promise<EraseReceipt> { return eraseImpl(this, id, opts); }
with `eraseImpl(gw: Gateway, ...)` living in the concern's module. The PUBLIC API stays byte-identical, `this` stays, there is no mixin chain to read through and no `prototype` assignment to defeat IDE navigation.
WHERE A BODY LANDS (DECIDED — coldstart caught this; do not guess): **extend the EXISTING module when the concern already has one; create a new module only where it does not.** Several concerns already own a module holding their vocabulary and readers — `erase.ts` (eraseClaims/eraseDefect/readTombstones), `renderers.ts`, `resolvers.ts`, `quarantine-pool.ts`, `adopt.ts`, `public.ts`, `registration.ts`, `trust.ts`. The gateway's method BODY for that concern belongs THERE, beside the vocabulary it already speaks — `Gateway.erase`'s body joins `erase.ts`, not a parallel `ops-erase.ts` twin. A `ops-*` shadow namespace would double the file count and split every concern across two files, which is the opposite of this ticket. Create NEW modules only for the concerns that have none today: the §14 write verbs, reads & subscriptions, lifecycle & binding, and the ingest doors — name them for the concern in the house style (short, no `ops-` or `-impl` suffix noise). If extending an existing module would make it incoherent (a vocabulary module swallowing 250 lines of orchestration), that is a judgment call the builder may make — but SAY SO in the PR and say why; do not split silently.
REJECTED, with reasons (do not 'improve' on this): MIXINS (`class Gateway extends WithErasure(Base)`) — forces every shared field from `private` to `protected` and makes the class's real shape unreadable at its declaration site; PROTOTYPE ASSIGNMENT + declaration merging — clever, and it breaks go-to-definition, which is exactly the property this ticket exists to buy; A FULL CONTEXT-OBJECT / DI REWRITE (each module takes a narrow `{reactor, backend, append, ...}` context) — genuinely better decoupling and NOT this ticket: it is a design change wearing a refactor's clothes, and it would make the diff unreviewable. If a module's dependency list turns out to be small and obvious, note it in a comment for a future ticket; do not build it here.

THE INTERNALS SEAM — the one real cost, and make it a feature. A module function reaching `gw.reactor`, `gw.backend`, `gw.operatorAuthor`, `gw.justPersisted`, `gw.quarantinePools`, etc. cannot use `private` fields from outside the class. Do NOT solve this by casting to `any`, and do NOT quietly widen every field to public. Instead declare ONE explicit, documented seam — an exported `GatewayInternals` interface (or a `/** @internal */`-marked field set) naming exactly what crosses — and have each module take it. The point: today `promote` silently touches `justPersisted` and nothing says so; after this, every module DECLARES what it reaches into, and an over-reaching module is visible in its signature. If a module needs a field that feels wrong for it to have, SAY SO in a comment — that is a real finding about coupling, and it is the kind of thing this refactor is supposed to surface.

SLICING — one concern per PR, each independently green, each its own review. Recommended order, most-isolated first so the pattern is established on the easy case and the hardest lands last against a proven seam:
  1. erasure · quarantine · promotion (T16's fix is already in it — the move carries a CORRECT implementation)
  2. renderers & serving
  3. §14 write verbs
  4. reads & subscriptions
  5. public door & versions
  6. ingest doors
  7. lifecycle & binding (most entangled; by now every other module's seam is known)
A slice that turns out NOT to be separable is NEWS, not a problem to force: stop, write down what couples it to the rest, and bring it back. A forced boundary is worse than the god-class, because it looks separated and isn't.

TWO DISCIPLINES, HELD HARD — these are what make a 2,000-line move safe:
  (1) **ZERO behavior change. Not one line.** No 'while I'm here' fixes, no renames, no signature tidying, no comment       rewrites beyond what the move requires, no reordering of statements. The 623 tests are the net, and a net only       works if the thing falling into it is a PURE MOVE. If you find a bug during the move — and you might; this is the       first careful read this code has had as a whole — DO NOT FIX IT. Author a ticket, cite the line, keep moving. A       refactor PR that also fixes something is a PR nobody can review.
  (2) **A frozen public-surface rail — and mind the trap coldstart caught.** The NAIVE version (freeze       `Object.getOwnPropertyNames(Gateway.prototype)` and exact-match it) IS BROKEN BY DESIGN: TypeScript's `private` is       compile-time only, so every private method sits on the prototype at runtime — and this refactor MOVES PRIVATE METHODS       OUT, so an exact-match list would fail on the first slice and get 'fixed' by editing the list every time, which is a       formality, not an invariant. Build it as a ONE-WAY assertion instead: freeze the list of PUBLIC methods (the API:       `open`/`boot`/`query`/`append`/`federate`/`register`/`erase`/`promote`/`openQuarantine`/`serveRoute`/`writeRoute`/      `serveBytes`/`mutateEntity`/`adoptions`/`surface`/… — derive the real list from the class, don't trust this sketch)       and assert every one of them STILL EXISTS on the prototype and is a function, i.e. `frozen ⊆ prototype`. Private       methods leaving is then invisible to the rail (correct — they are not the API), while a dropped or renamed public       method fails it (correct — that is the invariant: THE OUTSIDE CANNOT TELL THIS HAPPENED). The complementary       direction — no NEW public surface appearing — is not decidable at runtime for the same `private`-is-erased reason;       `test/cli/pack.test.ts` already pins the package's exported surface and covers what matters there, so do not invent a       second mechanism for it. Write this rail FIRST, before any slice, and keep it green through all seven.

RAILS: the prototype-surface rail (above) green through every slice; `npm run check` fully green per slice with the COUNT READ ALOUD (623 today — it must not drop, and a pure move should not raise it either, except for the new surface rail itself); the village green per slice (`demos/village/` — it exercises the composed system over real HTTP and is the end-to-end check a unit suite cannot be); and `git diff --stat` per slice should show near-symmetric insertions/deletions — a slice that ADDS meaningful net lines is not a move, and that asymmetry is the tell.

DO NOT: change any behavior (see discipline 1 — this is the ticket's whole safety argument); fix bugs found en route (author tickets); use mixins or prototype assignment (rejected above, with reasons); do the DI/context-object rewrite (named as separate, later, possibly-never); split `gql.ts` (635) or `rest.ts` (497) — they are already separate modules and are not this ticket; renumber or rename any public method; land more than one concern per PR; start before T16 is MERGED (its HIGH fix must be inside the erasure body this ticket moves — see the edge).

AFTER IT LANDS (housekeeping, not this ticket): narrow the `scope` arrays of T2 / T15 / T18 from `src/gateway/**` to the concern modules they actually touch. THAT is when `merge-forecast`'s vetoes drop and the certified width can rise — the side effect, arrived at honestly, after the reader already got what they came for.

CODE ANCHORS: `src/gateway/gateway.ts` (the whole file — 2,166 lines; class starts ~:160 with the private field block at :164-202); the existing already-separate neighbours to imitate for module shape: `src/gateway/erase.ts`, `src/gateway/adopt.ts`, `src/gateway/quarantine-pool.ts`, `src/gateway/renderers.ts`, `src/gateway/resolvers.ts`, `src/gateway/accounts.ts`, `src/gateway/trust.ts` (note the house style: a module owns a vocabulary + its readers, with a doctrine comment at the top — match it); `test/cli/pack.test.ts` pins the package's export surface and is the closest existing kin to the new prototype-surface rail.

SPEC: NONE. No behavior changes, so nothing in `spec/` moves — SPEC.md records what IS, and what IS is unchanged. This is the rare ticket that touches a lot of code and no spec. Its record is its JOURNAL entry.

VILLAGE: no new act — the village's job here is REGRESSION, not demonstration (there is nothing new to demonstrate; that is the point). Run it per slice and say so.

MIGRATION: none — no delta changes shape, no on-wire change, no at-rest change.

MERGE: touches the capability / erasure / federation / auth surfaces by construction (it moves them) -> Myk's merge (P6) per slice, never self-merge, however mechanical and however green. A refactor's danger is precisely that it looks safe.

DONE WHEN: every concern lives in its own module; `gateway.ts` is the class shell + its fields + thin delegating methods; the internals seam is ONE explicit documented thing; the prototype-surface rail proves the outside cannot tell; `npm run check` green with the count read aloud; the village green; and no behavior changed — provable by the diff being a move.
